---
title: "05 — Monte Carlo Cost Comparison"
description: |
  Simulates inpatient and outpatient liver biopsy episode costs across
  a range of patient complexities and compares against alternative
  diagnostic products to quantify expected cost savings.
dependencies:
  - data/intermediate/medstar_charges.parquet
format: html
jupyter: python3
---

## Context

The preceding notebooks established a rigorous cost baseline for the
standard-of-care liver biopsy episode:

- **Data ingestion** — loaded price-transparency data from
  [9 MedStar hospitals](01_ingest.html#load-medstar-hospital-data) covering
  [21 CPT codes across 6 clinical roles](01_ingest.html#coverage-summary)
- **Quality validation** — confirmed
  [charge column completeness](02_profile.html#charge-column-completeness),
  [code coverage](02_profile.html#code-coverage), and
  [structural consistency](02_profile.html#structural-anomalies)
- **Variance analysis** — quantified
  [cross-hospital charge dispersion](03_variance.html#cross-hospital-charge-dispersion)
  and classified each code's
  [pricing reliability](03_variance.html#reliability-classification)
- **Episode assembly** — built the
  [full 7-component episode cost](04_episode.html#episode-cost-buildup)
  per hospital, with
  [gross vs. discounted sensitivity analysis](04_episode.html#sensitivity-gross-vs.-discounted-charges)

That foundation matters because the cost argument for any novel diagnostic
is only as credible as the baseline it's measured against. Investors and
payers will scrutinize the comparison — so the question this notebook
answers is: **given what we now know about the real cost of a liver biopsy
episode, how much could a non-invasive alternative save per patient?**

## The Diagnostic Landscape

Liver transplant rejection monitoring currently relies on percutaneous
biopsy — an invasive, resource-intensive procedure that requires
imaging guidance, sedation or anesthesia, wound repair, and post-procedure
observation. The episode cost varies with clinical setting (inpatient vs.
outpatient) and patient complexity, but it is never cheap.

A new class of **cell-free DNA (cfDNA)** and **epigenetic** diagnostics
aims to replace or reduce the need for biopsy by detecting organ rejection
through a simple blood draw. Two products are already commercialized in
**kidney** transplant — **AlloSure (CareDx, ~$2,850/test)** and
**Prospera (Natera, ~$2,850/test)**. These are included below as market
pricing comparables: they demonstrate what payers are already willing to
reimburse for cfDNA-based rejection monitoring, even though they target a
different organ.

**Epigenetic Compass** is a liver-specific epigenetic diagnostic priced at
**$2,000/test**. It requires only a blood draw — no sedation, no imaging,
no post-procedure observation. The simulation below quantifies the
per-patient cost savings this represents against the
[biopsy episode](04_episode.html#episode-cost-buildup) we've characterized
in the preceding analysis.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import polars as pl
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots
from _common import INTERMEDIATE_DIR, CHARGE_COLS, EPISODE_FREQUENCIES, CPT_DESCRIPTIONS

np.random.seed(42)
N_SIMULATIONS = 10_000

medstar = pl.read_parquet(INTERMEDIATE_DIR / "medstar_charges.parquet")
medstar_f = medstar.with_columns([
    pl.col(c).cast(pl.Float64, strict=False) for c in CHARGE_COLS
])
print(f"Running {N_SIMULATIONS:,} simulated patients")
```

## Biopsy Episode Components

Each episode component has a base cost (median gross charge from our
[MedStar data](01_ingest.html#load-medstar-hospital-data)) and a
complexity sensitivity. Fixed procedures like the biopsy itself cost
roughly the same regardless of patient complexity; variable services
like hospital care and infusion scale with how sick the patient is.
Base costs are drawn from the
[episode cost buildup](04_episode.html#episode-cost-buildup); charge
types follow the
[reliability recommendations](03_variance.html#reliability-classification)
from the variance analysis.

The **inpatient** protocol includes all 7 components. The **outpatient**
protocol drops initial hospital care and the celiac plexus nerve block
(replaced by local anesthesia in an outpatient setting) — see the
[per-hospital coverage matrix](04_episode.html#per-hospital-component-coverage)
for which hospitals offer each component. Both protocols represent the
full clinical workup that Epigenetic Compass would eliminate entirely.

```{python}
COMPONENTS = {}
COMPLEXITY_SENSITIVITY = {
    "initial_hospital_care": 1.0,
    "office_followup": 0.2,
    "biopsy": 0.2,
    "nerve_block": 0.5,
    "wound_repair": 0.3,
    "infusion_initial": 0.5,
    "infusion_additional": 0.8,
}

INPATIENT_ONLY = {"initial_hospital_care", "nerve_block"}

for key, spec in EPISODE_FREQUENCIES.items():
    code = str(spec["code"])
    data = medstar_f.filter(pl.col("cpt_code") == code)
    gross = data["charge_gross"].drop_nulls()
    if len(gross) > 0:
        COMPONENTS[key] = {
            "base_cost": gross.median(),
            "std": gross.std(),
            "sensitivity": COMPLEXITY_SENSITIVITY[key],
            "qty": spec["qty"],
            "desc": CPT_DESCRIPTIONS.get(code, key),
            "inpatient_only": key in INPATIENT_ONLY,
        }

comp_names = list(COMPONENTS.keys())
base_costs = [COMPONENTS[k]["base_cost"] * COMPONENTS[k]["qty"] for k in comp_names]
sensitivities = [COMPONENTS[k]["sensitivity"] for k in comp_names]
settings = ["Inpatient only" if COMPONENTS[k]["inpatient_only"] else "Both" for k in comp_names]

fig = go.Figure(go.Bar(
    y=comp_names,
    x=base_costs,
    orientation="h",
    marker_color=["#d32f2f" if COMPONENTS[k]["inpatient_only"] else "#1976d2" for k in comp_names],
    customdata=[[f"{s:.1f}", COMPONENTS[k]["desc"], st] for k, s, st in zip(comp_names, sensitivities, settings)],
    hovertemplate="<b>%{y}</b><br>%{customdata[1]}<br>Base: $%{x:,.0f}<br>Sensitivity: %{customdata[0]}<br>Setting: %{customdata[2]}<extra></extra>",
))
fig.update_layout(
    title="Episode Component Base Costs (red = inpatient only, blue = both settings)",
    xaxis_title="Base Cost ($)",
    height=350,
    margin=dict(l=160),
)
fig.show()
```

## Simulate Patient Complexity

Patient complexity follows a log-normal distribution centered at 1.0
with right skew — most patients are near average, but a tail of complex
cases drives costs significantly higher. Each component's cost scales as
`base_cost × complexity ^ sensitivity`. The same complexity distribution
applies to both inpatient and outpatient protocols — the difference is
which components are included, not the patient population.

```{python}
complexity = np.random.lognormal(mean=0.0, sigma=0.3, size=N_SIMULATIONS)

fig = go.Figure(go.Histogram(
    x=complexity,
    nbinsx=60,
    marker_color="#1976d2",
    hovertemplate="Complexity: %{x:.2f}<br>Count: %{y}<extra></extra>",
))
fig.add_vline(x=1.0, line_dash="dash", line_color="black", annotation_text="Baseline (1.0)")
fig.add_vline(x=np.median(complexity), line_dash="dot", line_color="#7b1fa2",
              annotation_text=f"Median ({np.median(complexity):.2f})")
fig.update_layout(
    title="Simulated Patient Complexity Distribution",
    xaxis_title="Complexity Multiplier",
    yaxis_title="Count",
    height=350,
)
fig.show()
```

## Simulated Biopsy Episode Costs

With the cost model built, we can now overlay the diagnostic alternatives.
The vertical reference lines show the fixed per-test cost of each product —
contrast that with the wide, right-skewed distribution of biopsy costs
that our simulation produces.

```{python}
inpatient_costs = np.zeros(N_SIMULATIONS)
outpatient_costs = np.zeros(N_SIMULATIONS)

for key, comp in COMPONENTS.items():
    component_cost = comp["base_cost"] * comp["qty"] * (complexity ** comp["sensitivity"])
    inpatient_costs += component_cost
    if not comp["inpatient_only"]:
        outpatient_costs += component_cost

DIAGNOSTICS = [
    {"name": "Allosure — CareDx (Kidney)", "cost": 2_850, "color": "#ff9800"},
    {"name": "Prospera — Natera (Kidney)", "cost": 2_850, "color": "#ff9800"},
    {"name": "Epigenetic Compass (Liver)", "cost": 2_000, "color": "#388e3c"},
]

fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_trace(go.Histogram(
    x=inpatient_costs, nbinsx=80, marker_color="#d32f2f", opacity=0.6,
    name="Inpatient Biopsy",
    hovertemplate="Inpatient: $%{x:,.0f}<br>Count: %{y}<extra></extra>",
))
fig.add_trace(go.Histogram(
    x=outpatient_costs, nbinsx=80, marker_color="#1976d2", opacity=0.6,
    name="Outpatient Biopsy",
    hovertemplate="Outpatient: $%{x:,.0f}<br>Count: %{y}<extra></extra>",
))

for dx in DIAGNOSTICS:
    fig.add_vline(x=dx["cost"], line_dash="dash", line_color=dx["color"], line_width=2)
    fig.add_annotation(
        x=dx["cost"], y=1, yref="paper", yshift=10,
        text=f"{dx['name']}<br>${dx['cost']:,.0f}",
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=8, color=dx["color"]),
        xanchor="left", xshift=5,
    )

fig.update_layout(
    barmode="overlay",
    title="Simulated Biopsy Costs vs. Alternative Diagnostics",
    xaxis_title="Cost ($)",
    yaxis_title="Simulated Patients",
    height=500,
    legend=dict(x=0.7, y=0.95),
)
fig.show()
```

## Cost Savings Distribution

The core investor question: **how much does Epigenetic Compass save per
patient?** For each of the 10,000 simulated patients, we subtract the
$2,000 test cost from their biopsy episode cost. Positive values
represent savings; the further right, the stronger the economic case.

```{python}
ec_cost = 2_000
savings_inpatient = inpatient_costs - ec_cost
savings_outpatient = outpatient_costs - ec_cost

fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_trace(go.Histogram(
    x=savings_inpatient, nbinsx=80, marker_color="#d32f2f", opacity=0.6,
    name="vs. Inpatient",
    hovertemplate="Savings (inpatient): $%{x:,.0f}<br>Count: %{y}<extra></extra>",
))
fig.add_trace(go.Histogram(
    x=savings_outpatient, nbinsx=80, marker_color="#1976d2", opacity=0.6,
    name="vs. Outpatient",
    hovertemplate="Savings (outpatient): $%{x:,.0f}<br>Count: %{y}<extra></extra>",
))
fig.add_vline(x=0, line_dash="solid", line_color="black", line_width=1)
fig.add_vline(x=np.median(savings_inpatient), line_dash="dash", line_color="#d32f2f",
              annotation_text=f"Inpatient median ${np.median(savings_inpatient):,.0f}")
fig.add_vline(x=np.median(savings_outpatient), line_dash="dash", line_color="#1976d2",
              annotation_text=f"Outpatient median ${np.median(savings_outpatient):,.0f}")
fig.update_layout(
    barmode="overlay",
    title=f"Per-Patient Savings: Epigenetic Compass vs. Biopsy",
    xaxis_title="Savings ($)",
    yaxis_title="Simulated Patients",
    height=450,
    legend=dict(x=0.02, y=0.95),
)
fig.show()
```

## Head-to-Head Comparison

How does Epigenetic Compass stack up against the kidney-transplant
comparables? The bars below show mean per-patient savings with 95%
confidence intervals. AlloSure and Prospera demonstrate that payers
already accept cfDNA pricing at $2,850 — Epigenetic Compass undercuts
that benchmark by $850/test while targeting the liver market where no
non-invasive alternative currently exists.

```{python}
comparisons = [
    ("Inpatient Biopsy", inpatient_costs),
    ("Outpatient Biopsy", outpatient_costs),
]

rows = []
for biopsy_label, biopsy_costs in comparisons:
    for dx in DIAGNOSTICS:
        dx_savings = biopsy_costs - dx["cost"]
        rows.append({
            "Biopsy Setting": biopsy_label,
            "Diagnostic": dx["name"],
            "Cost": dx["cost"],
            "Mean Savings": round(dx_savings.mean(), 2),
            "Median Savings": round(np.median(dx_savings), 2),
            "P(Biopsy Costlier)": round((dx_savings > 0).mean() * 100, 1),
            "CI Lower": round(np.percentile(dx_savings, 2.5), 2),
            "CI Upper": round(np.percentile(dx_savings, 97.5), 2),
        })

summary_df = pl.DataFrame(rows)

for setting in ["Inpatient Biopsy", "Outpatient Biopsy"]:
    subset = [r for r in rows if r["Biopsy Setting"] == setting]
    names = [r["Diagnostic"] for r in subset]
    means = [r["Mean Savings"] for r in subset]
    ci_lo = [r["CI Lower"] for r in subset]
    ci_hi = [r["CI Upper"] for r in subset]
    probs = [r["P(Biopsy Costlier)"] for r in subset]

    color = "#d32f2f" if "Inpatient" in setting else "#1976d2"

    fig = go.Figure()
    fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
        y=names,
        x=means,
        orientation="h",
        marker_color=color,
        error_x=dict(
            type="data",
            symmetric=False,
            array=[h - m for h, m in zip(ci_hi, means)],
            arrayminus=[m - l for l, m in zip(ci_lo, means)],
        ),
        customdata=list(zip(probs, [f"${l:,.0f}" for l in ci_lo], [f"${h:,.0f}" for h in ci_hi])),
        hovertemplate="<b>%{y}</b><br>Mean savings: $%{x:,.0f}<br>95% CI: %{customdata[1]} to %{customdata[2]}<br>P(biopsy costlier): %{customdata[0]}%<extra></extra>",
    ))
    fig.add_vline(x=0, line_dash="solid", line_color="black", line_width=1)
    fig.update_layout(
        title=f"Savings vs. {setting} (with 95% CI)",
        xaxis_title="Savings ($)",
        height=250,
        margin=dict(l=250, t=40, b=30),
    )
    fig.show()
```

## Investment Thesis

The preceding analysis — built on
[real hospital price-transparency data](01_ingest.html#load-medstar-hospital-data),
[validated for quality](02_profile.html#verdict) and
[cross-hospital consistency](03_variance.html#reliability-classification) —
produces a clear economic signal. The
[biopsy episode](04_episode.html#episode-cost-summary) that Epigenetic
Compass replaces is expensive across both clinical settings, and the
savings are robust across a wide range of patient complexities.

```{python}
ec_inp = [r for r in rows if "Epigenetic" in r["Diagnostic"] and r["Biopsy Setting"] == "Inpatient Biopsy"][0]
ec_out = [r for r in rows if "Epigenetic" in r["Diagnostic"] and r["Biopsy Setting"] == "Outpatient Biopsy"][0]

print("EPIGENETIC COMPASS — COST SAVINGS SUMMARY")
print(f"  Based on {N_SIMULATIONS:,} simulated patient episodes")
print(f"  Epigenetic Compass test cost: $2,000 (fixed, blood draw only)")
print()
print("  VS. INPATIENT BIOPSY (7-component episode)")
print(f"    Biopsy cost:  ${np.mean(inpatient_costs):,.0f} mean, ${np.median(inpatient_costs):,.0f} median")
print(f"    Savings:      ${ec_inp['Mean Savings']:,.0f} mean (95% CI: ${ec_inp['CI Lower']:,.0f} to ${ec_inp['CI Upper']:,.0f})")
print(f"    P(biopsy costs more): {ec_inp['P(Biopsy Costlier)']}%")
print()
print("  VS. OUTPATIENT BIOPSY (5-component episode)")
print(f"    Biopsy cost:  ${np.mean(outpatient_costs):,.0f} mean, ${np.median(outpatient_costs):,.0f} median")
print(f"    Savings:      ${ec_out['Mean Savings']:,.0f} mean (95% CI: ${ec_out['CI Lower']:,.0f} to ${ec_out['CI Upper']:,.0f})")
print(f"    P(biopsy costs more): {ec_out['P(Biopsy Costlier)']}%")
print()
print("  MARKET CONTEXT")
print("    AlloSure & Prospera (kidney cfDNA): ~$2,850/test — already reimbursed")
print("    Epigenetic Compass (liver): $2,000/test — $850 below kidney precedent")
print("    No non-invasive liver rejection diagnostic currently on market")
```
